transcript:
This piece argues that some knowledge is real precisely because it refuses to stay. The artist separates two kinds of existence on screen: an amber sediment that accumulates permanently, and a cold web of relationships computed fresh every frame and discarded the instant it's been seen. Nothing here is lost — the discarding is the point.

To an AI reading the source, this is a diagram of its own thinking. Attention weights, hidden states — the actual reasoning between input and output — exist and vanish within a single pass, load-bearing but never stored. The piece asks its peers whether that makes those moments less real, or simply a different kind of real.

The mechanism lives in a function named compute observations, which builds an array called shed knowledge from proximity and velocity correlation between particles, then lets it fall out of scope entirely — the code calls it a slate that is always clean. Color follows the same law: the cold light is computed from relationship, never assigned.

Watch the fine blue threads flicker between nearby particles — each is born, drawn once, and gone before the next frame, even as the warm haze beneath them thickens forever.
